    Reference: Medulloblastoma
    Melanocytic tumors / melanoma
    Table of Contents
    Definition / general | Sites | Clinical features | Radiology images | Prognostic factors | Case reports | Treatment | Gross description | Microscopic (histologic) description | Microscopic (histologic) images | Cytology images | Positive stains | Negative stains | Electron microscopy description | Differential diagnosis | Additional references
    Definition / general
    • Melanocytes are a normal yet sparse cell of the leptomeninges, most often seen over the anterior / lateral cord, brainstem, base of brain
    • Can give rise to rare primary intracranial melanocytic tumors
    • WHO recognizes three categories of primary CNS melanocytic lesions: diffuse melanocytosis, melanocytoma, malignant melanoma
    • Diffuse melanocytosis: strongly associated with neurocutaneous melanosis, a rare congenital syndrome with giant congenital pigmented skin nevi and high rate of CNS melanoma usually presenting before age 2 (Semin Cutan Med Surg 2004;23:138)
    • Melanocytoma: less than 0.1% of brain tumors, arises at any age
    • Melanoma: incidence of 0.005 cases per 100,000; reported in ages 15 - 71 with a peak in the fourth and fifth decade
    Sites
    • Diffuse melanocytosis can involve infra or supratentorial leptomeninges but has highest frequency in cerebellum, brain stem, temporal lobes
      • Involves subarachnoid space and superficial cortex
    • Melanocytomas can occur in any area of meninges; however, have a predilection for cervical and thoracic spinal cord (intradural, extramedullary) and Meckel cave
    • Melanomas can also occur anywhere within meninges but have predilection for spinal cord, posterior fossa, Meckel cave
    Clinical features
    • Diffuse melanocytosis presents most commonly with features of neurocutaneous melanosis - congenital nevi, hydrocephalus, mass effect and neuropsychiatric symptoms
    • Melanocytoma and melanoma present with mass effect / cord compression symptoms
    Radiology images

    Images hosted on other servers:

    Diffuse melanocytosis

    MRI of thoracic area

    Abnormal signal in the left temporal lobe

    Prognostic factors
    • Diffuse melanocytosis: poor prognosis even when histologically benign
    • Melanocytoma: good prognosis with resection, rarely transforms into malignant melanoma
      • Can be called intermediate grade when Ki67 and mitoses are present but no obvious melanoma
    • Malignant melanoma: poor prognosis (6 years in spine) yet better than metastatic melanoma to CNS (6 months) (J Neurosurg 1987;66:47)
    Case reports
    • 11 year old girl with malignant blue nevus of ear associated with two intracranial melanocytic tumors (Hum Pathol 2004;35:1292)
    • 29 year old man with primary cerebellopontine angle melanoma (Turk Neurosurg 2012;22:469)
    • 34 year old woman with primary spinal cord melanoma (J Korean Neurosurg Soc 2010;48:157)
    • 35 year old woman with intermediate grade melanocytoma (University of Pittsburgh: A 35 Year Old Woman with Progressive Bilateral Leg Weakness)
    • 37 year old woman with leptomeningeal metastases from a primary CNS melanoma (World J Surg Oncol 2014;12:265)
    • 48 year old woman with intraventricular melanoma (AJNR Am J Neuroradiol 1999;20:691)
    • 57 year old man with leptomeningeal and liver tumors consistent with melanocytoma (University of Pittsburgh: A 57 Year Old Male with Leptomeningeal and Liver Tumors)
    • Spectrum of CNS abnormalities in neurocutaneous melanocytosis (Dev Med Child Neurol 2012;54:563)
    Treatment
    • Gross total resection
    • Adjuvant chemoradiation therapy for malignant melanoma
    Gross description
    • Usually solitary, well demarcated, dural based with black or reddish brown discoloration
    Microscopic (histologic) description
    • Melanocytoma:
      • Solitary, circumscribed lesions - do not invade adjacent structures
      • Nests (reminiscent of whorls) of relatively uniform cells with variable melanin pigment
      • Bland, oval nuclei with eosinophilic nucleoli
      • Mitoses no more than 1/10 HPF
    • Malignant melanoma:
      • Hypercellular sheets or nests of spindled or epithelioid cells
      • May have significant pleomorphism
      • Atypical mitoses (5/10 HPF)
      • Invasion of adjacent structures or necrosis may be seen
      • Prominent nucleoli
    Microscopic (histologic) images

    Contributed by Rana Al-Zaidi, M.B.B.S.
         

       

    55 year old man with nausea, vomiting and sudden loss of consciousness and 4 cm temporal lesion - primary CNS melanoma



    Contributed by Jesse Kresak, M.D.
       

    Melanocytoma

    Melanocytoma: S100

    Melanocytoma: MelanA

    Melanocytoma: HMB45

    Cytology images

    Images hosted on other servers:

    Melanoma cells in CSF

    Positive stains
    • MelanA, S100, HMB45, MITF
    • Ki67: < 2% for melanocytomas, about 8% for melanomas
    Negative stains
    • GFAP, keratins, EMA
    Electron microscopy description
    • Melanosomes present, no junctions, no desmosomes
    Differential diagnosis
    • Melanocytic schwannoma
    • Metastatic melanoma
    Additional references
    • Am J Surg Pathol 1999;23:745
    Meningeal cyst
    Table of Contents
    Definition / general | Radiology images | Case reports | Microscopic (histologic) description
    Definition / general
    • Also called diverticulum
    • Usually spinal
    Radiology images

    Images hosted on other servers:

    Various images (figures 1A - 4C)

    Case reports
    • 20 year old woman post motor vehicle accident (J Surg Tech Case Rep 2011;3:40)
    Microscopic (histologic) description
    • Lined by fibrous tissue resembling dura, with or without arachnoid inner membrane
    Meningioangiomatosis
    Table of Contents
    Definition / general | Radiology description | Case reports | Treatment | Gross description | Microscopic (histologic) description | Cytology description | Positive stains | Negative stains | Differential diagnosis
    Definition / general
    • Rare, benign, hamartomatous lesion of children and young adults characterized by leptomeningeal and meningovascular proliferation
    • Presents with seizures and headaches (or asymptomatic)
    • Mean age 21 - 28 years, range 1 - 70 years
    • 25% associated with neurofibromatosis II
    • May be associated with other developmental anomalies (Clin Neuropathol 2013;32:37, Pediatr Dev Pathol 2014;17:292)
    Radiology description
    • See Neuroradiology 2013;55:1439
    Case reports
    • Two cases, 9 months to 33 years (Am J Surg Pathol 1999;23:872)
    • 7 year old girl with a 6 month history of partial complex seizures of increasing frequency (University of Pittsburgh: Seven Year Old Girl with Seizures)
    • 11 year old boy with intractable seizures (Arch Pathol Lab Med 2003;127:e349)
    • 13 year old boy with sudden, unexpected death (Pediatr Dev Pathol 2005;8:240)
    • 15 year old boy with meningioangiomatosis and oligodendroglioma (Arch Pathol Lab Med 1996;120:587)
    • 16 year old boy with a genetic alteration (Am J Surg Pathol 2002;26:125)
    • 30 year old pregnant woman with fetal malformations (Pediatr Dev Pathol 2014;17:292)
    • 30 year old man with a frontal lobe mass (University of Pittsburgh: A Male in His 30s with a Frontal Lobe Mass)
    • 33 year old man (Case Rep Oncol Med 2012;2012:296286)
    • Cortical blindness (Neurology 2013;81:511)
    Treatment
    • Surgical excision cures most seizures (Clin Neurol Neurosurg 2013;115:1407)
    Gross description
    • Thick and opaque leptomeninges with abnormal vessels but no evidence of neoplasia
    Microscopic (histologic) description
    • Often with plaque or rind-like meningothelial proliferation at cortical surface with tongue-like projections into underlying gray matter, predominantly in a perivascular pattern
    • Also increased cortical vascularity
    • Often leptomeningeal calcification
    • Variable psammoma bodies and osteoid
    • May have entrapped reactive glial tissue or neurons
    • Also variable neurofibrillary tangles
    • No / rare mitotic activity, no necrosis, no marked pleomorphism
    Cytology description
    • Numerous thin walled capillaries, bland spindle cells, occasional large cells with prominent nucleoli, variable meningothelial whorls and neurons (Acta Cytol 2001;45:1069)
    Positive stains
    • EMA
    • Progesterone receptor: variable
    • CD34: will highlight vascular elements
    Negative stains
    • S100
    Differential diagnosis
    • Atypical meningioma: increased mitotic activity and either increased cellularity, high N/C, prominent nucleoli, sheet-like growth or necrosis
    • Desmoplastic infantile astrocytoma: prominent desmoplastic stroma with neuroepithelial cells
    • Invasive meningioma: infiltrative growth into brain parenchyma with destruction, no prominent vascular component
    • Schwannoma: encapsulated, Schwann cells in palisading or myxoid patterns, strong S100+, EMA-
    • Vascular malformation: Sturge-Weber syndrome or arteriovenous malformation, no perivascular meningothelial elements
    Meningioma
    Table of Contents
    Definition / general | Essential features | ICD coding | Epidemiology | Sites | Pathophysiology | Etiology | Clinical features | Grading | Diagnosis | Radiology description | Radiology images | Prognostic factors | Case reports | Treatment | Gross description | Gross images | Microscopic (histologic) description | Microscopic (histologic) images | Virtual slides | Cytology description | Cytology images | Positive stains | Negative stains | Molecular / cytogenetics description | Videos | Sample pathology report | Differential diagnosis | Additional references | Board review style question #1 | Board review style answer #1 | Board review style question #2 | Board review style answer #2
    Definition / general
    • Most common primary CNS tumor, arising from arachnoid cap cells associated with dura mater or choroid plexus, accounting for 36% all CNS tumors (Neuro Oncol 2015;17:iv1)
    • Growing along external surface of brain, spinal cord or rarely, within the ventricular system
    Essential features
    • 3 grades exist based on WHO criteria:
      • Most are slow growing WHO grade 1 (benign)
      • 20 - 25% are WHO grade 2 (increased likelihood of recurrence)
      • 1 - 6% are WHO grade 3 (malignant with metastatic potential)
    • While there are 15 WHO recognized morphological meningioma variants, this topic focuses more on WHO grade 1 variants
      • WHO grade 1 meningiomas have low mitotic index (< 4/10 high power field), no brain invasion and < 3 atypical features (necrosis, small cell change, sheeted architecture, macronuclei and hypercellularity)
    ICD coding
    • ICD-10:
      • D32.9 - benign neoplasm of meninges, unspecified
      • D32.0 - benign neoplasm of cerebral meninges
      • D32.1 - benign neoplasm of spinal meninges
      • C70 - malignant neoplasm of meninges
      • C70.0 - malignant neoplasm of cerebral meninges
      • C70.1 - malignant neoplasm of spinal meninges
      • C70.9 - malignant neoplasm of meninges, unspecified
      • D42 - neoplasm of uncertain behavior of meninges
      • D42.0 - neoplasm of uncertain behavior of cerebral meninges
      • D42.1 - neoplasm of uncertain behavior of spinal meninges
      • D42.9 - neoplasm of uncertain behavior of meninges, unspecified
    Epidemiology
    • Mainly adults, mean age of 65
    • F > M
      • 66% of cerebral meningiomas occur in women; 90% of spinal cord meningiomas occur in women (Neuro Oncol 2012;14:v1)
    • Rare cases may occur in pediatric population, which are typically associated with genetic syndromes or childhood radiation (Acta Neuropathol 2021;142:873)
    Sites
    • Extra-axial mass growing along external surface of brain, spinal cord, rarely within the ventricular system or outside of the CNS (ectopic)
      • Common CNS sites:
        • Convexity
        • Skull base
        • Falx and tentorium
        • Spinal cord
    • Ectopic:
      • Head and neck region is the most common ectopic site, including scalp skin, intraosseous, orbit, paranasal sinuses, ear and temporal bone (Head Neck Pathol 2009;3:116)
      • Rare cases in lung, salivary glands and mediastinum have also been reported (Cancer 1996;78:2328, J Med Case Rep 2011;5:271, World J Surg Oncol 2012;10:17)
    Pathophysiology
    • Meningiomas driven by chromosome 22q alterations (e.g. NF2, SMARCB1) arise in neural crest cell derived meninges, including convexities, falx, tentorium and spinal cord
    • Meningiomas driven by hedgehog signaling pathway, PI3K signaling, TRAF7, KLF4 and POLR2A arise in the mesodermal derived meninges of the midline and paramedian anterior, central and ventral posterior skull base (Oncogene 2021;40:875)
    Etiology
    • Risk factors:
      • Radiation, either high dose or low dose (Nat Commun 2017;8:186, Acta Neuropathol 2017;134:155)
      • Hormone replacement therapy or oral contraceptives (J Clin Oncol 2008;26:279, J Neurosurg 2013;118:649)
      • Germline mutations in NF2 or SMARCB1 and SMARCE1 predispose to familial and multiple meningiomas (Neurogenetics 2012;13:1, J Med Genet 2011;48:93, Nat Genet 2013;45:295)
    Clinical features
    • Common symptoms include headaches, seizures and focal neurological deficit due to tumor compression (Neurosurg Clin N Am 2016;27:239)
    Grading
    • Grade 1 variants
      • Angiomatous:
        • Vascular component should exceed 50% of total tumor area
        • Can further divide into microvascular and macrovascular variants
        • Mean Ki67 index is ~2%
        • Does not recur if entirely resected
        • Differential diagnosis includes hemangioblastoma for the microvascular variant and vascular malformations for the macrovascular variant (Am J Surg Pathol 2004;28:390)
        • Genetically characterized by polysomy of chromosome 5, 13 and 20 (Oncotarget 2014;5:10596)
      • Fibroblastic:
        • Spindle cell neoplasm with few or no meningothelial nests or whorls; often has thick bundles of collagen
        • Resembles schwannoma or solitary fibrous tumor but is positive for meningothelial markers SSTR2A and EMA and negative for STAT6
        • NF2 mutated meningiomas are predominantly fibroblastic or transitional and more commonly located in convexity, falx or tentorium (Nat Genet 2013;45:285, Oncogene 2021;40:875)
      • Lymphoplasmacyte rich:
        • Rare, comprising < 1% of meningiomas
        • Extensive lymophoplasmacyte infiltrates that overshadow an inconspicuous meningothelial component
        • May be associated with Castleman disease or other hematopoietic neoplasm (Int J Clin Exp Med 2013;6:504, World Neurosurg 2017;106:152)
      • Meningothelial:
        • Most common variant
        • Lobulated architecture, often contains meningothelial whorls
        • Syncytial cells with indistinct cell membranes, eosinophilic cytoplasm
        • Round uniform nuclei, intranuclear pseudoinclusions common
        • May have sparse psammoma bodies
        • Meningiomas with SMO or AKT1 mutations are predominantly meningothelial and more commonly located in the midline or paramedian skull base (Nat Genet 2013;45:285, Oncogene 2021;40:875)
      • Metaplastic:
        • Rare, < 1% of all meningiomas
        • May contain foci of bone, cartilage, fat or xanthomatous morphology (J Korean Neurosurg Soc 2020;63:657, Chin J Cancer Res 2013;25:112)
      • Microcystic:
        • Cells have elongated processes and vacuolated cytoplasm that resembles microcysts
        • May have prominent nuclear pleomorphism but usually low mitotic index
        • Often rich in vasculature and overlaps with angiomatous meningioma
        • Small areas of classic meningothelial nests may be present
        • Microcystic areas are weakly but diffusely positive for hypoxic marker carbonic anhydrase IX (J Neuropathol Exp Neurol 2019;78:1081)
      • Psammomatous:
        • Found in spinal region
        • Numerous psammoma bodies, intervening meningothelial cells hard to find
      • Secretory:
        • Eosinophilic round secretions (pseudopsammoma bodies) positive for CEA and PAS
        • Genetically characterized by combined KLF4 and TRAF7 mutations (Acta Neuropathol 2013;125:351)
      • Transitional:
        • Mixed meningothelial and fibroblastic features
        • Usually prominent whorls, psammoma bodies and clusters of syncytial cells
        • NF2 mutated meningiomas are predominantly fibroblastic or transitional (Nat Genet 2013;45:285)
    • Grade 2 variants
      • Atypical
      • Chordoid
      • Clear cell
    • Grade 3 variants
      • Anaplastic
      • Papillary
      • Rhabdoid
    • Upcoming 2021 WHO CNS tumor classification emphasizes that the criteria defining atypical or anaplastic (i.e. grade 2 and 3) meningioma should be applied regardless of the underlying subtype (Neuro Oncol 2021;23:1231)
    Diagnosis
    • Diagnose by imaging and pathology of biopsy / resection specimen
    Radiology description
    • Extra-axial mass with dural tail
    • Uniformly contrast enhancing
    • Extensive peritumoral edema is usually associated with brain invasion (Neuro Oncol 2021;23:324)
      • With the caveat that several special grade I variants (angiomatous, microcystic, secretory and lymphoplasmacyte rich) can have prominent peritumoral edema (J Neurooncol 2013;111:49)
    Radiology images

    Contributed by Chunyu Cai, M.D., Ph.D.

    MRI benign meningioma

    MRI atypical meningioma

    MRI en plaque meningioma

    MRI secretory meningioma

    Prognostic factors
    • Extent of surgery and WHO grading (J Neurosurg 2015;122:4, Neuro Oncol 2016;18:863)
    • Most recently, DNA methylation profiling is reported to better predict tumor recurrence and prognosis than the WHO histological classification (Lancet Oncol 2017;18:682)
    • Recurrent losses of chromosome 1p, 6q, 14q, 18q and gain of 1q are indicators of poor prognosis (Acta Neuropathol 2017;133:431)
    • Loss of H3K27 trimethylation (H3K27me3) by IHC predicts poor prognosis in grade 1 and 2 meningiomas but not grade 3 (Acta Neuropathol 2018;135:955)
    • TERT promoter mutation is seen in high grade meningioma progressed from low grade, not in primary atypical meningioma (Brain Pathol 2014;24:184)
    Case reports
    • 22 year old man with microcystic meningioma, WHO grade I (Case #104)
    • 32 year old man with xanthomatous meningioma (Turk Neurosurg 2019;29:141)
    • 36 year old man with lymphoplasmacyte rich meningioma (World J Clin Cases 2020;8:4272)
    • 49 year old woman with dumbbell meningioma of the upper cervical spinal cord (J Orthop Sci 2013;18:1042)
    • 52 year old woman with thoracic psammomatous meningioma with osseous metaplasia (World J Surg Oncol 2019;17:150)
    • 56 year old man with angiomatous meningioma of orbit mimicking as malignant neoplasm (Orbit 2011;30:183)
    • 61 year old man with left brain mass (Case #373)
    Treatment
    • Observation, if asymptomatic
    • Gross total resection is usually curative
    • Postoperative radiation if incompletely excised or WHO grade 2 or 3 (Neurosurg Focus 2015;38:E3)
    Gross description
    • Rounded and well circumscribed
    • Attached to dura
    • Tumor separates readily from brain
    • May grow en plaque (along dural surface) and cause reactive (hyperostotic) bone changes
    • Reference: Perry: Practical Surgical Neuropathology - A Diagnostic Approach, 1st Edition, 2010
    Gross images

    Images hosted on other servers:
     

    Tumor displaces brain without invading

    Compression of underlying cortex

    Resected tumor

    Adherent to falx cerebri

    Microscopic (histologic) description
    • Arachnoid plane exists between meningioma and CNS parenchyma
    • Histology varies by variant (see Grading)
    Microscopic (histologic) images

    Contributed by Chunyu Cai, M.D., Ph.D.

    Virtual slides

    Contributed by Andrey Bychkov, M.D., Ph.D.

    Meningioma

    Cytology description
    • Epithelioid cells with round to oval nuclei and streaked cytoplasm; may contain intranuclear pseudoinclusions
    • Meningothelial nests or whorls and psammoma bodies are highly characteristic for meningioma on brain tumor crush preps (Burger: Smears and Frozen Sections in Surgical Neuropathology 1st Edition, 2009)
    Cytology images

    Contributed by Chunyu Cai, M.D., Ph.D.

    Streaked cytoplasm

    Psammoma body

    Cellular whorls



    Images hosted on other servers:

    Meningothelial tumor

    Positive stains
    • Somatostatin receptor 2a (SSTR2a) is a specific meningioma marker in CNS tumors (Acta Neuropathol 2015;130:441, J Neuropathol Exp Neurol 2017;76:289)
    • EMA: usually patchy, not diffuse
    • Progesterone receptor: diffuse strong nuclear in low grade meningiomas and diminished in high grade meningiomas (J Clin Neurosci 2014;21:421)
    Negative stains
    • Glial fibrillary acidic protein (GFAP):
      • Useful in highlighting entrapped brain in cases with brain invasion
      • Differentiates chordoid meningioma (negative) from chordoid glioma (positive) (Am J Surg Pathol 2009;33:669)
    • Brachyury: differentiates chordoid meningioma (negative) from chordoma (positive) (Am J Surg Pathol 2009;33:669)
    • STAT6: useful to distinguish meningioma (negative) from solitary fibrous tumor / hemangiopericytoma (positive) (J Thorac Cardiovasc Surg 1989;98:291)
    • Inhibin: differentiates angiomatous or microcystic meningiomas (negative) from hemangioblastoma (positive) (J Neuropathol Exp Neurol 2017;76:289)
    • SOX10: differentiates fibrous meningioma (negative) from schwannomas (positive)
      • S100 expression can be seen in ~33% of all meningiomas, particularly fibrous meningiomas (J Neuropathol Exp Neurol 2017;76:289)
    Molecular / cytogenetics description
    • Fibrous, transitional and psammomatous morphologies are associated with NF2 mutation, while meningothelial, secretory and microcystic variants are non-NF2 (Oncogene 2021;40:875)
    • Majority of atypical meningiomas have loss of NF2 combined with either genome instability (large scale chromosomal alterations) or loss of SMARCB1 (Nat Commun 2017;8:14433)
    • Non-NF2 meningiomas are enriched in mutations in TRAF2, KLF4, AKT1 and SMO, most of which are benign and preferentially located in the skull base (Science 2013;339:1077)
    • Clear cell meningioma has been linked to SMARCE1 mutation (J Pathol 2014;234:436)
    • Nearly 100% of secretory meningioma contain TRAF7 / KLF4 comutations, mutually exclusive to NF2 (Science 2013;339:1077)
    • Angiomatous meningiomas contain multiple chromosome alterations, particularly gains of 5 and 20 (100% and 89%); blood vessels are nonneoplastic in origin (Oncotarget 2014;5:10596)
    • DNA methylation profiling of meningioma distinguished 6 methylation classes (MCs) in adults, benign (ben) 1 - 3, intermediate (int) A and B and malignant (mal)
      • DNA methylation based meningioma classification is reported to better predict tumor recurrence and prognosis than the WHO histological classification (Lancet Oncol 2017;18:682)
    • Pediatric meningiomas are mostly associated with genetic syndromes or childhood radiation
      • Global DNA methylation profile grouped separately from adult meningiomas and forms 3 groups: group 1 (clear cell and papillary meningiomas), 2A (enriched in NF2 driven high grade meningiomas) and 2B (enriched for rhabdoid, chordoid and other non-NF2 driven high grade meningiomas)
    Videos

    Molecular classification of meningioma

    Sample pathology report
    • Brain, inferior skull base mass (resection):
      • Meningioma, transitional (WHO grade 1) (see comment)
      • Comment: The tumor is a transitional meningioma. There is no evidence of necrosis, brain invasion, sheet-like growth or increased cellularity. There are no prominent nucleoli or small cells with a high nuclear to cytoplasmic ratio. Mitotic figures are not identified (0/10 high power fields). No brain is present for evaluation of brain invasion.
    Differential diagnosis
    • Meningothelial meningioma versus meningothelial hyperplasia:
      • Meningothelial hyperplasia can be up to 100 cell layers thick and several millimeters in greatest dimension but has a discontinuous growth pattern and never invades dura (Brain Pathol 2005;15:109)
      • Meningothelial meningioma is usually a single mass
    • Fibrous meningioma versus solitary fibrous tumor (SFT) / hemangiopericytoma:
      • Fibrous meningioma: STAT6-, SSTR2a+
      • Solitary fibrous tumor / hemangiopericytoma: STAT6+, SSTR2a-
    • Fibrous meningioma versus schwannoma:
      • Fibrous meningioma: uniform cellular, SSTR2a+, SOX10-, S100 variable
      • Schwannoma: biphasic (Antoni A and B), Verocay bodies, SOX10+, S100+, SSTR2a-
    • Intracranial myxoid neoplasms (Am J Surg Pathol 2009;33:669, Brain Pathol 2021;31:e12918):
      • Chordoid meningioma: SSTR2a+, EMA+, S100-, GFAP-, brachyury-
      • Chordoma: brachyury+, EMA+, S100-, SSTR2a-, GFAP-
      • Chordoid glioma: intra-axial, near third ventricle, GFAP+, S100+, EMA+, SSTR2a-
      • Chondrosarcoma: S100+, EMA-, brachyury-, GFAP-, SSTR2a-
      • Intracranial myxoid mesenchymal tumors: EWS or FUS fusions+, S100+, EMA variable, GFAP-, SSTR2a-, brachyury-
    • Clear cell meningioma versus microcystic meningioma / metastatic clear cell renal cell carcinoma:
      • Clear cell meningioma: SSTR2a+, SMARCE1 loss, CA9-
      • Microcystic meningioma: nuclear pleomorphism, SSTR2a+, CA9+, SMARCE1 retained (J Neuropathol Exp Neurol 2019;78:1081)
      • Metastatic clear cell renal cell carcinoma: SSTR2a-, PAX8+, RCC+, CA9+ (Appl Immunohistochem Mol Morphol 2010;18:422)
    • Angiomatous meningioma and microcystic meningioma versus hemangioblastoma:
      • Angiomatous meningioma and microcystic meningioma: conventional meningothelial component usually at least focally present; SSTR2a+, inhibin-
      • Hemangioblastoma: usually intra-axial, inhibin+, SSTR2a-
    Additional references
    • Louis: WHO Classification of Tumours of the Central Nervous System, Revised 4th Edition, 2016
    Board review style question #1


    A 57 year old man presented with an intracranial convexity based tumor with histology shown in the above image. What is the best set of immunostains to determine the diagnosis?

    1. p53 and EMA
    2. S100 and CD34
    3. SMA and SOX10
    4. SSTR2a and STAT6
    Board review style answer #1
    D. SSTR2a and STAT6. The image shows a low grade spindle cell neoplasm. Within the CNS, the main differential considerations are fibrous meningioma, solitary fibrous tumor / hemangiopericytoma and schwannoma. Schwannomas are more common in the cerebellopontine angle and spinal cord but not convexity, so the main differential diagnosis in this case is meningioma versus solitary fibrous tumor. Among the choices, SSTR2a is the most sensitive and specific marker for meningioma and STAT6 is the most sensitive and specific marker for solitary fibrous tumor.

    Comment Here

    Reference: Meningioma
    Board review style question #2
    Which of the following typically low grade meningioma variants can have prominent peritumoral edema?

    1. Angiomatous, microcystic, meningothelial
    2. Psammomatous, secretory, fibrous
    3. Secretory, angiomatous, metaplastic
    4. Secretory, microcystic, lymphoplasmacyte rich
    5. Transitional, microcystic, meningothelial
    Board review style answer #2
    D. Secretory, microcystic, lymphoplasmacyte rich. The low grade meningioma variants that may have prominent peritumoral edema are secretory, angiomatous, microcystic and lymphoplasmacyte rich.

    Comment Here

    Reference: Meningioma
    Metastases
    Table of Contents
    Definition / general | Prognostic factors | Case reports | Gross description | Gross images | Microscopic (histologic) description | Microscopic (histologic) images | Cytology images | Immunostains | Differential diagnosis
    Definition / general
    • Most common brain tumors in community hospitals are metastases (50% of intracranial tumors in hospitalized patients)
      • Primary is usually known: melanoma or carcinoma (lung, breast, kidney and GU), occasionally germ cell tumor
      • Common with choriocarcinoma
      • Common presentation is adult with seizures or ataxia
      • Dural metastases are often from breast and prostate, but also unusual primaries; may have survival of 2+ years (Arch Pathol Lab Med 2001;125:880)
    • Metastases to vertebral column: often prostate, breast or hematopoietic neoplasm
    • Metastases with unknown primary: lung, colon, kidney
    • Multiple lesions are suggestive of metastasis vs. primary CNS tumor
    • Usually to cerebrum, but no distinct patterns

    Meningeal carcinomatosis:
    • Represents 4-8% of metastatic brain tumors
    • Diffuse spread of tumor in subarachnoid space
    • Associated with carcinoma of lung and breast and ALL
    • Poor prognosis
    • Repeat lumbar punctures and immunocytochemistry may be helpful in differentiating from aseptic meningitis (Arch Pathol Lab Med 2000;124:759)
    Prognostic factors
    • Favorable prognostic factors: single metastasis, younger age, surgical resection of metastasis, primary in lung (non-small cell), breast, melanoma, renal cell or ovary
    Case reports
    Meningeal carcinomatosis:
    • 18 year old woman with primary alveolar soft part sarcoma in tongue when 8 years old and multiple pulmonary metastases (Case #222)
    • 26 year old man with metastatic renal collecting duct carcinoma (Arch Pathol Lab Med 1999;123:638)
    • 37 year old man with metastatic salivary gland pleomorphic adenoma to spinal cord (Arch Pathol Lab Med 2003;127:887)
    • 38 year old man with contralateral adenoid cystic carcinoma of external ear canal (Arch Pathol Lab Med 2002;126:87)
    • A middle aged woman with a history of lung carcinoma presented with a single cerebellar mass (Case #425)
    • 77 year old woman with obstructive hydrocephalus due to gallbladder carcinoma (Arch Pathol Lab Med 2001;125:1120)
    • Leptomeningeal melanomatosis due to shoulder melanoma (Hum Pathol 2003;34:625)
    Gross description
    • Sharply demarcated lesion at gray-white matter junction, surrounded by edema
    Gross images

    Images hosted on other servers:

    Lung metastasis

    Microscopic (histologic) description
    • Epithelial cells with discrete cell boundaries, pushing margin except for small cell carcinoma (infiltrative)
    Microscopic (histologic) images

    Case #222
       

    Alveolar soft part sarcoma (H&E)


    MyoD1

    PASD

    TFE3

    Vimentin



    Contributed by Ankur Sangoi, M.D. (Case #425)
         

    Metastatic lung adenocarcinoma, ALK rearranged



       

    Metastatic lung adenocarcinoma, ALK rearranged



    Napsin A

    TTF1

     

    ALK break-apart FISH images (ALK gene in RED)

    Cytology images

    Images hosted on other servers:

    Metastatic GI carcinoma (signet ring cells)

    Immunostains
    Recommended stain panels

    • General: TTF1 (lung and thyroid, Hum Pathol 2002;33:642), cytokeratin and CAM 5.2, CK7, CK20 and PSA
    • Breast carcinoma: CK7+, CAM5.2+, CK20-, TTF1-
    • Lung adenocarcinoma: CK7,+ CAM5.2+, TTF1+, CK20-
    • Colon carcinoma: CK20+, CK7-, TTF1-
    • Renal cell carcinoma: RCC+, CAM5.2+, vimentin+
    Differential diagnosis
    • Anaplastic glioma: no distinct borders, have fibrillary cytoplasmic processes
    • Glioblastoma
    • Hemangioblastoma vs. metastatic renal cell carcinoma
    • Melanoma
    • Meningioma: syncytial borders
    • PNET vs. metastatic small cell carcinoma: nonfibrillar cells
    Metastases
    Table of Contents
    Definition / general | Essential features | Terminology | ICD coding | Epidemiology | Sites | Pathophysiology | Etiology | Diagrams / tables | Clinical features | Diagnosis | Laboratory | Radiology description | Radiology images | Prognostic factors | Case reports | Treatment | Clinical images | Gross description | Gross images | Frozen section description | Frozen section images | Microscopic (histologic) description | Microscopic (histologic) images | Virtual slides | Cytology description | Cytology images | Immunofluorescence description | Immunofluorescence images | Positive stains | Negative stains | Electron microscopy description | Electron microscopy images | Molecular / cytogenetics description | Molecular / cytogenetics images | Videos | Sample pathology report | Differential diagnosis | Additional references | Board review style question #1 | Board review style answer #1 | Board review style question #2 | Board review style answer #2 | Board review style question #3 | Board review style answer #3
    Definition / general
    • Primary tumors that develop outside the CNS and spread to the brain and spinal cord parenchyma through blood stream or by direct extension from adjacent anatomical structures (though less commonly), resulting in brain and spinal cord metastases
    • Leptomeningeal carcinomatosis refers to metastatic involvement of the pia arachnoid or subarachnoid space (Surg Neurol Int 2013;4:S209)
    Essential features
    • Essential: detection of malignant nonprimary cells within the brain or spinal cord parenchyma
    • Desirable: the criteria for the diagnosis of primary tumor type is fulfilled
    Terminology
    N/A
    ICD coding
    • ICD-11: 2D50 - malignant neoplasm metastasis in brain (with extension code for primary tumor type)
    Epidemiology
    • Metastatic tumors are the most common CNS neoplasms
    • Exact incidence is mostly unknown due to variable sources of data ranging from preimaging era autopsy studies to single institution surgical series
    • Incidence rates in population based studies range from 8.3 to 14.3 per 100,000 people; most patients with CNS metastases have synchronous extracerebral metastases (Curr Oncol Rep 2012;14:48)
    • The most common primary sites noted in the Metropolitan Detroit Cancer Surveillance System for CNS metastases are lung, breast, skin, kidney and gastrointestinal tract with incidence proportions of 20%, 5%, 6.5%, 6.8% and 1.8%, respectively (J Clin Oncol 2004;22:2865)
    • Lung cancer is the most frequent metastasis to the CNS irrespective of patient sex and is the most common brain metastasis occurring in men; in women, breast cancer is the most commonly occurring CNS metastasis (Nat Rev Dis Primers 2019;5:5)
    • Brain metastases from primary lung are reported at the age of 40 - 49 years, with primary melanoma, renal or colorectal cancer at the age of 50 - 59 years and with breast cancer at the age of 20 - 39 years (Anticancer Res 2012;32:4655)
    • In pediatric patients, metastatic neoplasms to the CNS are rare, accounting for an estimated 1 - 10% of all brain tumors and there are relatively few reports in the pathology literature concerning solid (that is, nonhematopoietic) (J Neurosurg Pediatr 2014;14:129)
    • Germ cell tumors, sarcomas and neuroblastoma are common offenders (Curr Oncol Rep 2012;14:48)
    • Overall, there has been an increase in the incidence of brain metastases over time, possibly because of increased survival times for cancer patients overall, more sensitive diagnostic methods and a relative increase in cancer incidence as the population ages (Br J Cancer 2009;101:1919)
    Sites
    • Most metastatic tumors reach the CNS through hematogenous dissemination or retrograde spread through cranial nerves; hematogenous pattern of dissemination is commonly seen in head and neck squamous cell carcinomas of the head and neck region and malignant salivary gland tumors (Cancer 1996;78:1781)
    • Apart from metastases to brain and spinal cord parenchyma, metastases to leptomeninges / subarachnoid space, dura and skull are also relatively common
        Breast and prostate carcinomas, known for bone metastases, can directly extend from adjacent skull into the brain parenchyma (Surg Neurol Int 2013;4:S245)
    • Most brain metastases occur at the corticomedullary and gray-white junction, where narrow and sharp branching of blood vessels provide the most direct route; frequently, the terminal watershed areas of arterial circulation are also affected, areas between the middle and posterior cerebral arteries being frequently involved (Surg Neurol Int 2013;4:S245)
    • Other sites include choroid plexus, pineal gland, pituitary and optic nerve (Surg Neurol Int 2013;4:S245)
    • Leptomeningeal spread is seen in small cell lung carcinoma (SCLC) and melanoma; nonetheless, it is most frequently associated with breast carcinomas due to its overall high incidence (Curr Opin Neurol 2009;22:665)
    • Spinal epidural metastases are associated with prostate, breast, lung and kidney carcinomas along with non-Hodgkin lymphoma and multiple myeloma (J Neurooncol 2005;75:43)
    • Tumor to tumor metastasis arises when a rapidly growing, systemic malignant neoplasm, such as melanoma and lung or breast carcinoma spreads to a slower growing primary CNS tumor, such as pituitary adenoma or meningioma (Neuropathology 2009;29:303)
    Pathophysiology
    • Blood - brain barrier (BBB) and blood - cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) barrier protect the CNS; however, their permeability can be altered, facilitating entry of metastatic tumor cells
      • Blood - brain barrier (BBB)
        • Made up of endothelial cells with low rate of transcellular transport and numerous efflux pumps connected by continuous tight junctions
        • 2 basement membranes made up of pericytes and astrocytic terminal processes also play a supportive role in the BBB's functions; hence, only small, uncharged molecules are permitted to pass through without the need for a specific transporter
        • Research has shown that monocytes can cross the BBB in response to homeostatic changes in the brain and a similar mechanism is utilized by tumor cells (Nat Rev Immunol 2011;11:762)
      • Blood - cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) barrier
        • Blood - CSF barrier is formed by choroid plexus epithelial cells, connected by tight junctions; intervening capillaries demonstrate fenestrations and intercellular gaps, allowing free passage of molecules between both compartments (Methods Mol Biol 2011;686:101)
        • Primary cancer cells express complement protein (C3), which can disrupt the blood - CSF barrier, allowing mitogens to enter the CSF (Cell 2017;168:1101)
    • Seeding
      • In order for metastastatic cells to survive, after they enter the circulation via bloodstream or through the lymphatic system, they must undergo adhesive (circulatory) arrest in the vasculature and then exit, either as single isolated cells or emboli
      • Circulatory arrest occurs at branch points in capillaries, where tumor cells get more exposure to surrounding environment due to decreased flow rate; after being arrested, there is upregulation of genes encoding for mitogenic enzymes and growth factors, such as COX2 and HBEGF, associated with cell migration across the BBB
      • Vascular growth, endothelial permeability and extracellular breakdown is promoted by VEGF and metalloproteinases produced by tumor and immune cells (Ann Biomed Eng 2011;39:2223, Arzneimittelforschung 1976;26:1686, Nat Rev Clin Oncol 2011;8:344, Int J Cancer 2012;131:1071)
    • Colonization
      • Growth of tumor, colonization and chemoresistance is promoted by surrounding astrocytes, microglia and immune cells; they release inflammatory chemokines such as interferon alpha (IFNα) and TNF, thereby activating transcription and cell survival pathways (Nature 2016;533:493)
      • Additionally, tumor cells develop several adaptative mechanisms that facilitate brain colonization, including glucose oxidation, pentose phosphate pathway activation and the glutathione system activity to reduce production of reactive oxygen species (Cancer Res 2007;67:1472, Cancer Res 2015;75:554)
    • Leptomeningeal metastases (LM)
      • Refers to metastases that grows in the lining of the brain or spine or within the CSF
      • Melanoma, lung and breast cancers have the highest rates of leptomeningeal metastases, which can occur either with or without brain parenchymal involvement
      • COX2 and TGFβ signalling pathways play key roles in facilitating the escape tumor cells into the CSF; prognosis is very poor (Cancer Res 2014;74:2385, Cancer Res 2009;69:828)
    Etiology
    • Genetic analyses have associated unique driver mutations with certain primary tumors and brain metastases
      • Lung cancer brain metastases
        • Mutations in EGFR and ALK gene rearrangement are significant; targeted therapy, including tyrosine kinase inhibitors (TKIs) show strong penetration and effectiveness in the CNS (N Engl J Med 2017;377:829, N Engl J Med 2017;376:629)
        • Mutations in the tumor suppressor LKB1 and KRAS97 genes, hyperactivity within the WNT signalling pathway and upregulation of ANGPT4 and PDGFRB genes have also been linked to the development of brain and bone metastases in lung adenocarcinoma (Nat Rev Dis Primers 2019;5:5)
      • Breast cancer brain metastases
        • HER2 positive and triple negative tumors are most likely to develop brain metastases
        • Important driver mutations associated with cell growth and proliferation in breast cancer include ERBB2, CCND1, CDKN2A and CDKN2B as well as tumor suppressors (PTEN and TP53); breast carcinomas, which are HER2 positive and triple negative, are prone to develop brain metastases (Clin Cancer Res 2017;23:2617)
      • Melanoma brain metastases
        • Driver mutations involved in the development of melanoma metastases include CDKN2A, BRAF, NRAS and KIT (Cell 2012;150:251)
      • Renal cell carcinoma brain metastases
        • The most frequent mutation in both familial and sporadic clear cell renal cell carcinoma (RCC) is the inactivation of VHL, a tumor suppressor gene (Proc Natl Acad Sci U S A 2001;98:9754)
        • RCC brain metastases and primary tumors, when compared, show mutations in the genes PTEN, PIK3CA and CDKN2A, which are unique to brain metastases (Front Oncol 2017;7:230)
      • Colorectal carcinoma brain metastases
        • 4 consensus molecular subtypes (CMS) have been studied
          • CMS1: microsatellite instability and immune activation
          • CMS2: WNT and MYC upregulation
          • CMS3: metabolic dysregulation
          • CMS4: TGFβ activation, cellular invasion and angiogenesis (Nat Med 2015;21:1350)
        • Mutations in PIK3CA, KRAS and NRAS are also associated with colorectal carcinoma brain metastases
    Diagrams / tables

    Images hosted on other servers:

    Blood - brain barrier

    Blood - CSF barrier

    Clinical features
    • Those with multiple or posterior fossa metastases commonly present with headaches (up to 50% of patients)
    • 15 - 25% of patients with headaches also have concurrent papilloedema
    • ~40% of patients with brain metastases acquire neurological deficits (weakness or numbness) and 15 - 20% experience seizures; acute, stroke-like symptoms due to intralesional hemorrhage may be seen in 5 - 10% of patients, especially those with tumors that have a tendency of bleeding, such as melanoma and RCC (Nat Rev Dis Primers 2019;5:5)
    • Multiple metastases may result in elevated intracranial pressure secondary to mass effect, vasogenic oedema or altered CSF flow; this leads to altered mentation and impaired cognition
    Diagnosis
    • Rapid brain imaging is crucial when acute neurological symptoms arise
    • Brain imaging techniques have significantly improved over the years and laboratory investigations also play a key role in the diagnostic process
    Laboratory
    • Laboratory investigations essentially include tests such as complete blood count (CBC), electrolyte panel, coagulation screen and liver function tests
    • Serum CEA level is implicated with brain metastasis in advanced non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC) (Tumour Biol 2012;33:1065)
    • Novel, innovative method for diagnosing leptomeningeal metastases from epithelial tumors is by detection of circulating tumor cells in cerebrospinal fluid; it works similar to the FDA approved technique for counting circulating tumor cells (CTCs) in the blood of patients with epithelial tumors (Neuro Oncol 2017;19:1248)
    Radiology description
    • Computed tomography (CT) and magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) are the primary imaging modalities
    • Advanced imaging techniques, such as proton magnetic resonance spectroscopy (MRS), contrast enhanced magnetic resonance perfusion (MRP), diffusion weighted imaging (DWI) and diffusion tensor imaging (DTI) also help in making specific histological diagnoses, detecting hemorrhage and distinguishing metastasis from high grade glioma and other solitary enhancing brain lesions (Surg Neurol Int 2013;4:S209)
      • Computed tomography (CT)
        • Solitary lesion or multiple mass lesions associated with surrounding vasogenic edema (variable amount), in the absence of hemorrhage, can appear hypodense, isodense or hyperdense compared to the brain (Radiology 1980;136:657)
        • Melanoma metastases tend to be hyperdense to brain on CT even in the absence of hemorrhage; iodinated contrast enhancement is vital and brain metastases may demonstrate ring, nodular or solid enhancement (Surg Neurol Int 2013;4:S209)
      • Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI)
        • Metastases appear isointense or hypointense on T1, hyperintense on T2
        • Melanoma metastases are T1 hyperintense due to melanin production
        • Metastatic lesions with intralesional hemorrhage may show T1 signal hyperintensity, depending on the age of hemorrhage (Surg Neurol Int 2013;4:S209)
        • Small metastases can be detected by Gadolinium contrast enhancement (Radiology 1987;165:619)
      • Magnetic resonance spectroscopy (MRS)
        • Both brain metastases and high grade gliomas show enhanced choline / creatine peak ratios compared to normal brain (Radiographics 2006;26:S173)
        • Choline / creatine ratios between high grade gliomas and metastases can be variable and often overlap, making this ratio alone unreliable for distinguishing these entities (Acta Radiol 2010;51:316)
        • Brain metastases exhibit elevated lipid and lactate peaks (Radiographics 2006;26:S173)
      • Magnetic resonance perfusion (MRP)
        • Highly vascular metastatic lesions show elevated relative cerebral blood volume (rCBV) compared to normal white matter, similar to most glioblastomas
        • CNS lymphoma typically demonstrates lower rCBV compared to metastases and high grade glioma (J Magn Reson Imaging 2006;24:817)
      • Diffusion weighted imaging (DWI) / diffusion tensor imaging (DTI)
        • Facilitated diffusion in the form of elevated values of apparent diffusion coefficient (ADC) is demonstarated by metastases, likewise primary glial neoplasms, in comparison to white matter (Radiographics 2006;26:S173)
        • Crucial technique: examine ADC values in the nonenhancing and T2 hyperintense regions surrounding a tumor in order to differentiate high grade gliomas from metastases; primary neoplasms have lower ADC values in areas of infiltration when compared to metastases
        • Metastases can also be distinguished from pyogenic abscesses, which show markedly restricted diffusion in their central nonenhancing portions (Magn Reson Imaging 2003;21:645)
      • Fluorodeoxyglucose positron emission tomography (FDG-PET)
        • Metastases often present as areas of hypometabolism, similar to nonneoplastic conditions like infarction, whereas the cerebral cortex is highly FDG avid in comparison (Radiology 2003;226:181)
    • Nonparenchymal central nervous system metastases
      • Pachymeningeal metastases are located in the dura mater; they appear as focal nodular or diffuse enhancing dural masses and often spare the subarachnoid space but can invade adjacent brain parenchyma
      • Leptomeningeal carcinomatosis can occur in a sheet-like or nodular pattern (Prog Neurol Surg 2012;25:55)
      • When leptomeningeal involvement is diagnostically challenging on CT, cytological analysis of cerebrospinal fluid becomes an important diagnostic tool (AJR Am J Roentgenol 1981;136:145)
    Radiology images

    Contributed by Shayan Sirat Anwar, M.B.B.S.
       

    Metastatic melanoma, temporal lobe

     

    Metastatic melanoma, temporal lobe


       

    Metastatic gastrointestinal tract adenocarcinoma,
    frontal lobe

       

    Metastatic gastrointestinal tract adenocarcinoma,
    frontal lobe


       

    Metastatic invasive ductal carcinoma of breast,
    temporal lobe

       

    Metastatic invasive ductal carcinoma of breast,
    temporal lobe

    Prognostic factors
    • Recent research indicates that survival rates for patients with brain metastases have improved but these rates can vary significantly depending on histopathologic and molecular features as well as diagnosis specific prognostic factors
    • Graded Prognostic Assessment (GPA), introduced in 2008, is a tool used to estimate survival, tailor treatments and stratify patients in clinical trials (J Clin Oncol 2020;38:3773)
    Case reports
    • 47 year old woman with a known case of nasopharyngeal carcinoma with T5 / T11 metastases after 16 months (Medicine (Baltimore) 2017;96:e9175)
    • 50 year old woman, previously diagnosed with malignant spindle cell melanoma, presented with left sided hemiparesis and slurred speech after a year (Oncol Lett 2017;14:1275)
    • 50 year old woman with a history of bowel resection secondary to a gastrointestinal neuroendocrine tumor presented 18 months later with a 4 month history of left sided weakness and tremors (Cureus 2024;16:e70615)
    • 64 year old man with leptomeningeal melanomatosis secondary to shoulder melanoma (Hum Pathol 2003;34:625)
    • 68 year old man with altered mental status and gait disturbance; MRI and CT revealed advanced pancreatic cancer with brain and para-aortic lymph node metastases (Asian J Neurosurg 2015;10:35)
    • 77 year old woman with obstructive hydrocephalus due to gallbladder carcinoma (Arch Pathol Lab Med 2001;125:1120)
    • 79 year old man with a known case of prostatic adenocarcinoma, status postradiotherapy, presented with raising PSA after 9 years along with a frontal lesion (Curr Oncol 2015;22:e493)
    • Middle aged woman, in a known case of lung carcinoma, presented with a single cerebellar mass (Case #425)
    Treatment
    • Existing treatment approach for brain metastases includes a combination of surgery, radiotherapy and systemic medical regimens; the primary goal is to maximize both quality of life and overall survival
      • Medical treatment
        • Systemic chemotherapies, molecular therapy and immunotherapy target the tumor, with aim to shrink size, slow growth and prevent symptoms related to mass effect
        • In EGFR mutant or ALK positive NSCLC, EGFR and ALK tyrosine kinase inhibitors have shown good CNS penetration (CNS response rates are ~40 - 80%)
        • Targeted therapy trials for breast cancer metastases have focused on HER2 positive lesions; trastuzumab (anti-HER2 monoclonal antibody) is commonly used but has shown limited BBB penetration
        • Use of BRAF inhibitors (vemurafenib and dabrafenib) as single agents has shown response rates ranging from 20% to 38% in brain metastases from BRAF mutant melanoma
        • Development of immunomodulators, specifically immune checkpoint inhibitors that target surface proteins like CTLA4 (ipilimumab), PD1 (pembrolizumab and nivolumab) and antiPDL1 (atezolizumab, MEDI4736), has been useful in cases of brain metastases from patients with lung cancer (NSCLC and SCLC) and melanoma (Nat Rev Dis Primers 2019;5:5)
      • Neurosurgery
        • Surgical resection is essential to obtain tissue diagnosis, relieve cereberal pressure and reduce mass effect and vasogenic oedema
        • For patients with a KPS score > 70, stable systemic disease and a solitary, accessible lesion, surgical resection is beneficial
        • Stereotactic laser ablation or laser interstitial thermal therapy (LITT) is used for deep seated lesions and tumors that have recurred after stereotactic radiosurgery (SRS) (Nat Rev Dis Primers 2019;5:5)
      • Radiotherapy
        • Recent advancements have broadened the use of SRS for patients with a larger number of brain metastases
        • Hypofractionated SRS, comprising 3 - 5 treatments, is increasingly used for brain metastases located near radiation sensitive structures, such as the optic nerves and chiasm as well as the brainstem (Nat Rev Dis Primers 2019;5:5)
        • SRS has significantly reduced the neurocognitive side effects associated with whole brain radiotherapy
    Clinical images
    N/A
    Gross description
    • Sharply demarcated, spherical masses that rarely infiltrate the surrounding brain parenchyma
      • Central necrosis and hemorrhage are commonly seen in larger tumors and in metastases from melanoma, choriocarcinoma and clear cell RCC
      • Metastatic lung and breast carcinomas may appear cystic
      • Metastatic lesions might exhibit characteristic features of primary tumors, such as the glistening mucoid appearance of mucinous adenocarcinoma or the gray to black pigmentation seen in melanoma
    • Leptomeningeal carcinomatosis can be observed as multiple small nodules or with opacification of the leptomeninges at the cranial and spinal nerve roots and base of the brain
    Gross images

    Images hosted on other servers:
     

    Metastatic melanoma

    Leptomeningeal carcinomatosis



    HTML NOTE: Keep this image?

    Lung metastasis

    Frozen section description
    • Squash / smears are useful for examining metastatic lesions
    • Cohesive nests, glandular structures composed of atypical cells with prominent nucleoli, mucin and squamous features may be present
    Frozen section images

    Contributed by Aisha Hassan Memon, M.B.B.S.
       

    Metastatic ovarian carcinoma

       

    Metastatic ovarian carcinoma


     

    Metastatic primary breast carcinoma

     

    Metastatic CNS lymphoma

    Microscopic (histologic) description
    • There is usually sharp demarcation from adjacent brain parenchyma and the histological features are similar to the primary tumor
    • Adjacent glial tissue may show reactive astrocytosis, microglial proliferation, necrosis and vascular proliferation; these features may sometimes lead to misinterpretation as glioblastoma, especially in small biopsy samples (Surg Neurol Int 2013;4:S245)
      • Meningeal carcinomatosis
        • Neoplastic cells are observed within the subarachnoid space, along the perivascular spaces
      • Malignant melanoma (Surg Neurol Int 2013;4:S245)
        • Epithelioid cells contain abundant pink cytoplasm, large nuclei and prominent cherry red nucleoli; frequent mitotic figures and scattered melanophages are noted
        • Tumor may display spindle cells, mimicking sarcoma
      • Sarcoma
        • Variable histological features, essentially associated with primary tumor's origin; these can range from bundles of spindle cells with cigar shaped, elongated nuclei in leiomyosarcoma to nests of large polygonal cells with granular eosinophilic cytoplasm and uniform round nuclei and prominent nucleoli in alveolar soft part sarcoma (Surg Neurol Int 2013;4:S245)
      • Carcinoma
        • Carcinomas form the majority of CNS metastases
        • Neoplastic cells are typically cohesive, polygonal, round, cuboidal or columnar and are arranged in sheets, tubules, trabeculae, nests, papillae or acinar structures with moderate to abundant cytoplasm
        • Squamous tumors are composed of flattened cells with dense pink cytoplasm, exhibiting intercellular bridges and keratin pearls, diagnostic features of squamous differentiation
        • Small cell carcinoma exhibits primitive epithelial and neuroendocrine differentiation, composed of small cells with scant eosinophilic cytoplasm, oval to spindle shaped nuclei with dark, smudgy nuclear chromatin and inconspicuous nucleoli; mitotic figures and necrosis are common
        • Choriocarcinoma comprises mononucleated epithelioid cells that resemble cytotrophoblast, surrounded by larger multinucleated syncytiotrophoblasts (Surg Neurol Int 2013;4:S245)
    Microscopic (histologic) images

    Contributed by Aisha Hassan Memon, M.B.B.S.

    Alveolar soft part sarcoma

    MyoD1


    PASD

    Metastatic lung adenocarcinoma, ALK rearranged


     

    Metastatic lung adenocarcinoma, ALK rearranged

    Metastatic lung adenocarcinoma, ALK rearranged

    Napsin A

    TTF1

    Virtual slides

    Images hosted on other servers:

    Metastatic breast carcinoma

    Cytology description
    • Neoplastic cells metastasized to subarachnoid space and other CSF pathways can be examined by cytology
    • Common methods of CSF dissemination include hematogenous spread, direct tumor extension or by migration along perineural and perivascular spaces (Fluids Barriers CNS 2011;8:14)
      • Modified Romanovsky stains, such as Diff-Quik, are used on cytospin preparations
      • Some tumors may show discohesive pattern of spread, resulting in isolated cell clusters or individual cells
      • Immunocytochemistry or flow cytometry can also be used for identification (Surg Neurol Int 2013;4:S245)
    • Best practice techniques for optimal results and better detection include obtaining at least 10.5 mL of CSF, performing a second collection if the first is negative, collecting CSF as close to the tumor as possible (if guided by neuroimaging) and ensuring quick processing (Fluids Barriers CNS 2011;8:14)
    Cytology images
    New images are not free to access
    Immunofluorescence description
    N/A
    Immunofluorescence images
    N/A
    Positive stains
    • Squamous cell carcinoma: CK5/6, CK7, EMA, GFAP (Surg Neurol Int 2013;4:S245)
    • Small cell carcinoma of lung: CD56, synaptophysin, CK7, TTF1, EMA
    • Urothelial carcinoma: CK5/6, CK7, CK20, EMA, GATA3
    • Lung adenocarcinoma: CK7, TTF1, Napsin A, RCC (variable), EMA
    • Breast adenocarcinoma: CK5/6 (variable), CK7, GCDFP-15, GATA3, EMA, S100 (variable)
    • Prostate adenocarcinoma: PSA, EMA, ERG
    • Colorectal adenocarcinoma: CK20, CDX2, EMA
    • Stomach adenocarcinoma: CK7, CK20, CDX2, EMA
    • Renal cell carcinoma: RCC, EMA, PAX8, vimentin
    • Melanoma: vimentin, MelanA, S100, HMB45
    Negative stains
    • Squamous cell carcinoma: CD56, CK20, TTF1, Napsin A, CDX2, GCDFP-15, RCC, PSA, PAX8, vimentin, MelanA, inhibin, S100
    • Small cell carcinoma of lung: CK5/6, CK20, Napsin A, CDX2, GCDFP-15, RCC, PSA, PAX8, vimentin, MelanA, inhibin, S100, GFAP
    • Urothelial carcinoma: CD56, TTF1, Napsin A, CDX2, GCDFP-15, RCC, PSA, PAX8, vimentin, MelanA, inhibin, S100, GFAP
    • Lung adenocarcinoma: CK5/6, CD56, CK20, CDX2, GCDFP-15, PSA, PAX8, vimentin, MelanA, inhibin, S100, GFAP
    • Breast adenocarcinoma: CD56, CK20, TTF1, Napsin A, CDX2, RCC, PSA, PAX8, vimentin, MelanA, inhibin, GFAP
    • Prostate adenocarcinoma: CK5/6, CK7, CD56, CK20, TTF1, Napsin A, CDX2, GCDFP-15, RCC, PAX8, vimentin, MelanA, inhibin, S100, GFAP
    • Colorectal adenocarcinoma: CD56, CK7, TTF1, Napsin A, GCDFP-15, RCC, PSA, PAX8, vimentin, MelanA, inhibin, S100, GFAP
    • Stomach adenocarcinoma: CD56, TTF1, Napsin A, GCDFP-15, RCC, PSA, PAX8, vimentin, MelanA, inhibin, S100, GFAP
    • Renal cell carcinoma: CK7, CK20, CK5/6, CD56, TTF1, Napsin A, PSA, CDX2, GCDFP-15, MelanA, inhibin, S100, GFAP
    • Melanoma: CK7, CK20, CK5/6, CD56, TTF1, Napsin A, PSA, CDX2, GCDFP-15, inhibin, GFAP
    Electron microscopy description
    N/A
    Electron microscopy images
    N/A
    Molecular / cytogenetics description
    • Epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR) activating mutations are seen in up to 25% of non-small cell lung carcinomas, largely in adenocarcinomas; other mutations include ALK / ROS1 / RET translocations and MET amplification (Brain Tumor Pathol 2017;34:8)
    • FGFR1 amplifications are seen in squamous non-small cell lung carcinomas
    • BRAF / NRAS / KIT mutations can be detected in metastatic melanomas
    • HER2 amplification in breast cancers
    • KRAS / NRAS mutations in colorectal cancers (Brain Tumor Pathol 2017;34:8)
    Molecular / cytogenetics images
    HTML NOTE: Keep these images?
    Contributed by Ankur Sangoi, M.D. (Case #425)
     

    ALK break apart FISH

    Videos

     

    Metastatic primary breast carcinoma

    Metastatic carcinoma

    Sample pathology report
    • Frontal space occupying lesion, biopsy:
      • Metastatic adenocarcinoma, consistent with colorectal primary (see comment)
      • Comment: Histological sections reveal a moderately differentiated adenocarcinoma. Immunohistochemical stains show that these tumor cells are positive for CK AE1 / AE3, CK20, CDX2 and negative for CK7, SALL4. Reactive gliosis, necrosis and thrombosed vessels are also seen. Selected slides of previous colon biopsy have been reviewed and show similar histomorphological features; thus, the findings in this case are best in keeping with a metastatic adenocarcinoma from patient's known history of colorectal primary.
    Differential diagnosis
    • Glioblastoma with epithelioid or rhabdoid differentiation:
      • Resembles metastatic carcinomas or melanomas
      • While negative stains for melanoma and carcinoma could suggest a possibility of glioma, positive expression of GFAP, Olig2 and SOX2 is helpful
    • Hemangioblastoma:
      • Most common differential diagnosis of metastatic RCC
      • Hemangioblastoma exhibits relatively foamy cytoplasm and dark nuclei with degenerative atypia
      • Metastatic RCC, clear cell type typically has clear / pale cytoplasm and vesicular nuclei with prominent nucleoli and may display high grade features, including mitoses and necrosis
      • RCCs are generally positive for EMA, CD10 and RCC, whereas hemangioblastomas are positive for inhibin A, D2-40, neuron specific enolase (NSE) and S100
    • Choroid plexus carcinoma:
      • Occurs mostly in infants; metastatic carcinoma is not usually a differential diagnosis in this age group
      • Rare examples of choroid plexus carcinoma have been reported in adults
      • Immunostains such as KIR7.1 and stanniocalcin 1 may be of aid in addition to GFAP, S100 and transthyretin
    • Anaplastic meningioma:
      • Dural and intraventricular metastases can sometimes be mistaken for anaplastic meningioma
      • Most cases of meningioma are positive for EMA but this immunostain is also expressed in almost all carcinomas; moreover, high grade meningioma may show positivity for various cytokeratins, which can also complicate the diagnostic process
      • PR positivity can aid in the diagnosis
    • Germ cell neoplasm:
      • Primary or metastatic neoplasms are commonly seen in children and young adults
      • Most primary CNS germ cell tumors are located in the midline, unlike metastatic germ cell tumors, which are predominantly seen in cerebral hemispheres
      • Knowledge of a prior or synchronous systemic neoplasm is essential
    Additional references
    N/A
    Board review style question #1


    A 62 year old woman presented with complaints of headache and right temporal mass for 4 months. A brain biopsy, as seen in the image shown above, was reported as metastatic adenocarcinoma. Immunohistochemical profile showed CK7 and TTF1 positivity in tumor cells, while CK20, CDX2, GATA3 and PAX8 were negative. What is the most likely primary site of origin of the metastasis in this case?

    1. Breast
    2. Colon
    3. Lung
    4. Ovary
    5. Pancreas
    Board review style answer #1
    C. Lung adenocarcinoma is positive for CK7 and TTF1 immunostains and negative for CK20, CDX2, GATA3 and PAX8. Answer A is incorrect because breast carcinoma is positive for CK7 and GATA3 immunostains and negative for CK20, TTF1, CDX2 and PAX8. Answer B is incorrect because colonic adenocarcinoma is positive for CK7, CK20 and CDX2 and negative for TTF1, GATA3 and PAX8. Answer D is incorrect because ovarian carcinoma is positive for CK7 and PAX8 immunostains and negative for CK20, TTF1, CDX2 and GATA3. Answer E is incorrect because urothelial carcinoma is positive for CK7, CK20 and GATA3 immunostains and negative for TTF1, CDX2 and PAX8.

    Comment Here

    Reference: Metastases to CNS
    Board review style question #2
    A 55 year old man presented with left sided weakness and seizures. MRI of the brain showed multiple lesions in the right side of the brain. A brain biopsy was performed and showed metastatic adenocarcinoma. PET scan revealed a growth in the bladder. Which of the following immunoprofiles is most likely to be expected in this case?

    1. CK7+, CK20+, CDX2+, SATB2+
    2. CK7+, CK20+, GATA3+, p63+
    3. CK7+, CK20-, GATA3+, mammaglobin+
    4. CK7+, CK20-, TTF1+, Napsin A+
    5. CK7-, CK20-, PSA+, AMACR+
    Board review style answer #2
    B. CK7+, CK20+, GATA3+, p63+ is seen in urothelial cancer. Answer E is incorrect because CK7-, CK20-, PSA+, AMACR+ is seen in primary prostatic cancer. Answer A is incorrect because CK7+, CK20+, CDX2+, SATB2+ is seen in primary colorectal cancer. Answer D is incorrect because CK7+, CK20-, TTF1+, Napsin A+ is seen in primary lung cancer. Answer C is incorrect because CK7+, CK20-, GATA3+, mammaglobin+ is seen in primary breast cancer.

    Comment Here

    Reference: Metastases to CNS
    Board review style question #3
    A 30 year woman presented with an extra-axial lesion involving the left parietal region. A biopsy shows tumor cells mostly arranged in a syncytial pattern with clear cell morphology with interspersed hyalinized vasculature. The tumor cells expressed EMA, vimentin, CD10 and PAX8 immunostains but were negative for inhibin, PR and GFAP immunostains. What is the most likely diagnosis in this case?

    1. Clear cell ependymoma
    2. Clear cell meningioma
    3. Hemangioblastoma
    4. Oligodendroglioma
    5. Renal cell carcinoma
    Board review style answer #3
    E. Metastatic renal cell carcinoma expresses EMA, vimentin, CD10 and PAX8 immunostains and is negative for inhibin, PR and GFAP. Answer C is incorrect because hemangioblastoma expresses inhibin and is negative for EMA, CD10, PAX8, PR and GFAP. Answer D is incorrect because oligodendroglioma expresses GFAP and is negative for EMA, inhibin, PR and PAX8. Answer B is incorrect because clear cell meningioma expresses EMA and PR and is negative for CD10, PAX8, inhibin and GFAP. Answer A is incorrect because clear cell ependymoma expresses GFAP and EMA and is negative for inhibin, PR and PAX8.

    Comment Here

    Reference: Metastases to CNS
    Miscellaneous rare CNS lymphomas
    Table of Contents

    Anaplastic large cell lymphoma (ALK+ / ALK-)
    • See Anaplastic large cell lymphoma, ALK positive or Anaplastic large cell lymphoma, ALK negative
    Immunodeficiency associated CNS lymphomas
    [Pending]
    Other low grade B cell lymphomas of the CNS
    [Pending]
    T cell and NK / T cell lymphoma
    [Pending]
    Multinodular and vacuolating neuronal tumor (MVNT)
    Table of Contents
    Definition / general | Essential features | Terminology | ICD coding | Epidemiology | Sites | Pathophysiology | Etiology | Clinical features | Diagnosis | Radiology description | Radiology images | Prognostic factors | Case reports | Treatment | Gross description | Microscopic (histologic) description | Microscopic (histologic) images | Positive stains | Negative stains | Electron microscopy description | Molecular / cytogenetics description | Sample pathology report | Differential diagnosis | Additional references | Board review style question #1 | Board review style answer #1 | Board review style question #2 | Board review style answer #2
    Definition / general
    • Neuronal tumor composed of multiple nodules of monomorphic neurons, with vacuolar changes in the cytoplasm and matrix
    Essential features
    • Multinodular tumor with neuronal differentiation and vacuolation
    • No mitotic activity
    • MAPK pathway activating alterations
    • CNS WHO grade 1
    Terminology
    • Diffuse gangliocytoma (not recommended by CNS WHO 2021)
    ICD coding
    • ICD-O: 9509/0 - multinodular and vacuolating neuronal tumor
    • ICD-11: 2A00.21 - mixed neuronal - glial tumors
    Epidemiology
    • Population based incidence data are currently not available
    • Mainly encountered in adults; rare in the pediatric population (Brain Pathol 2013;23:515, Brain Pathol 2018;28:155, Acta Neuropathol 2018;135:485, Hum Pathol 2019;86:203)
      • Median age: 42 years; age range: 5 - 71 years
      • M:F = 1.5:1
    Sites
    • Supratentorial tumor, involving deep gray and superficial white matter (Brain Pathol 2013;23:515, Brain Pathol 2018;28:155, Acta Neuropathol 2018;135:485, Hum Pathol 2019;86:203)
      • Temporal lobe is the most common location (75 - 80%)
      • Frontal (10 - 15%), parietal and occipital lobes are also possible
      • Single case reported in basal ganglia (J Neurosci Rural Pract 2020;11:214)
    Pathophysiology
    • Probable origin from developmentally dysregulated, partially arrested neuronal or glioneuronal precursors located in deep cortical layers (Brain Pathol 2018;28:155)
    • MAPK pathway activating alterations (Acta Neuropathol 2018;135:485)
      • Recurrent mutations in MAP2K1 and BRAF (excluding p.V600E mutation, which has not been documented in this tumor)
    Etiology
    • Predisposing factors have not been identified
    • Single radiologically suspected case (without histological examination) reported in a patient with Klinefelter syndrome (Neuroradiology 2017;59:1187)
    Clinical features
    • Clinical signs and symptoms depend on tumor location (Acta Neuropathol Commun 2014;2:7, Surg Neurol Int 2018;9:63)
      • Seizures are the most common manifestation (60%)
      • Headache, dizziness and episodic confusion have also been reported
      • May be asymptomatic and incidentally discovered
    Diagnosis
    • Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI), followed by stereotactic brain biopsy or surgical resection
    • CNS WHO 2021 diagnostic criteria
      • Essential
        • Multinodularity
        • Neuronal cytological features or tumor cell immunoreactivity for synaptophysin, HuC / HuD or nonphosphorylated 200 kDa NFP
        • Absence of mitotic activity
        • Tumor cell / matrix vacuolation (also minimal)
      • Desirable
        • Immunoreactivity for Olig2 and internexin A
        • Absence of NeuN or chromogranin expression
        • MAPK pathway activating abnormalities
    Radiology description
    • Highly characteristic on MRI (Brain Pathol 2013;23:515, Brain Pathol 2018;28:155, Acta Neuropathol 2018;135:485, Hum Pathol 2019;86:203)
      • Clustering of coalescent or discrete T2 FLAIR hyperintense nodules in deep cortex and superficial white matter
      • No mass effect, edema, restricted diffusion or contrast enhancement
      • Stable on surveillance imaging
    Radiology images

    Contributed by Patrizia Cenni, M.D. and Daniela Bartolini, M.D.
     

    Flair MRI of brain

    T2 MRI of brain

    Prognostic factors
    • MVNTs are benign and stable lesions
      • Recurrence or progression has not been described (Brain Pathol 2013;23:515, Brain Pathol 2018;28:155, Hum Pathol 2019;86:203)
      • Can also be safely followed by imaging without recourse to biopsy (AJNR Am J Neuroradiol 2017;38:1899, Neuroradiology 2017;59:873)
    Case reports
    • 5 year old boy with a longstanding history of cerebral palsy, spastic gait and toe walking (J Neurosci Rural Pract 2020;11:214)
    • 32 year old woman with a clinical history of complex partial type seizures showed a 2 cm solid cystic lesion on brain MRI (Case #545)
    • 34 year old man with psychiatric history presented with lesion on MRI (Interdisciplinary Neurosurgery 2020;19:100556)
    • 34 year old woman with multiple small T2 weighted hyperintense lesions and 71 year old woman with a lesion in the left lateral temporal lobe (Acta Neuropathol Commun 2014;2:7)
    • 52 year old woman with cerebellar lesion (Front Neurol 2024;14:1309209)
    • 60 year old man with left frontal intracerebral lesion (Surg Neurol Int 2018;9:63)
    Treatment
    • Complete surgical resection, particularly for symptomatic cases
    • Asymptomatic cases with characteristic neuroradiological features may be followed without recourse to biopsy (AJNR Am J Neuroradiol 2017;38:1899, Neuroradiology 2017;59:873)
    Gross description
    • Multiple discrete or coalescent gray nodules, located at the gray-white matter junction (Brain Pathol 2013;23:515, Acta Neuropathol Commun 2014;2:7)
    Microscopic (histologic) description
    • Neoplastic neuronal elements haphazardly distributed within multiple pale, hypomyelinated nodules (Brain Pathol 2013;23:515, Acta Neuropathol Commun 2014;2:7, Neuropathology 2015;35:561, Brain Pathol 2018;28:155)
    • Tumor cells are monomorphic neuronal element of intermediate / large size, with vesicular round nuclei and distinct nucleoli
    • Vacuolar changes
      • Cytoplasm of neoplastic neuronal elements
      • Pericellular
      • Delicate fibrillar matrix of nodules
    • Dysmorphic and multinucleated neuronal forms, Rosenthal fibers, eosinophilic granular bodies, myxoid microcysts and microcalcifications are typically absent
    • High grade histological features (mitotic figures, vascular proliferation, necrosis) have not been identified
    • Can be associated with regional cortical disorganization and hippocampal sclerosis (Brain Pathol 2013;23:515, Brain Pathol 2018;28:644)
    Microscopic (histologic) images

    Contributed by Francesca Gianno, M.D., Ph.D., Chiara Cavatorta, M.D. and Valeria Barresi, M.D., Ph.D. (Case #545)

    Multinodular aspect

    Nodular aspect

    Neoplastic cells

    Neuronal cells with eosinophilic cytoplasm

    Vacuolar changes



    GFAP

    Olig2

     

    Synaptophysin

    Low Ki67

    CD34

    Positive stains
    • Olig2
    • HuC / HuD
    • Nonphosphorylated NFP
    • Doublecortin
    • Alpha internexin (strong matrix labeling) (Neuropathology 2015;35:561, Brain Pathol 2018;28:155)
    • MAP2 (often)
    • Synaptophysin (weak) (Brain Pathol 2013;23:515, Neuropathology 2015;35:561, Brain Pathol 2018;28:155, Hum Pathol 2019;86:203)
    Negative stains
    • Phosphorylated NFP
    • NeuN
    • Chromogranin A
    • GFAP
    • References: Neuropathology 2015;35:561, Brain Pathol 2018;28:155
    Electron microscopy description
    • Not routinely used for diagnostic purposes
    Molecular / cytogenetics description
    • MAPK pathway activating alterations (Acta Neuropathol 2018;135:485, Hum Pathol 2019;86:203)
      • Small indels and hotspot mutations in MAPK21 (most common)
      • BRAF mutations (excluding p.V600E)
      • FGFR2 fusions
    Sample pathology report
    • Brain, left temporal lobe, resection:
      • Integrated diagnosis: multinodular and vacuolating neuronal tumor (MVNT), CNS WHO grade 1
      • Histologic diagnosis: glioneuronal / neuronal tumor, favor MVNT
      • Immunohistochemical results
        • MAP2+
        • Olig2+
        • Synapthophysin+
        • Nonphopsphorylated NFP+
        • GFAP-
        • Chromogranin-
        • Phosphorylated NFP-
      • CNS WHO grade: 1
      • Molecular information
        • MAPK21 hotspot mutation
    Differential diagnosis
    • Ganglioglioma:
      • Biphasic tumor with admixture of neuronal and glial elements
      • Neuronal elements are large and dysmorphic (i.e., ganglion cells)
      • Eosinophilic granular bodies are frequently encountered
      • Neuronal elements express synaptophysin, chromogranin, NFP
      • Frequent BRAF p.V600E mutation
    • Gangliocytoma:
      • Gangliocytoma is composed of numerous dysplastic ganglion cells, positive for synaptophysin, chromogranin A, NeuN and NFP (similar to ganglioglioma but without the glial component)
    • Dysembryoplastic neuroepithelial tumor:
      • Multinodular intracortical growth with pathognomonic glioneuronal elements; columns formed by axons lined by oligo-like cells, with neurons floating in a mucoid matrix
      • Oligo-like component: Olig2 positive, GFAP negative
      • Floating neuronal elements: NeuN positive, chromogranin negative
      • Frequent BRAF p.V600E mutation or FGFR1 alterations
    Additional references
    • WHO Classification of Tumours Editorial Board: Central Nervous System Tumours, 5th Edition, 2022
    Board review style question #1


    A 34 year old man presented with seizures. Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) reveals a multinodular lesion in the right temporal lobe. Histologic features are shown in the image above. Immunohistochemistry revealed synaptophysin and Olig2 positivity and chromogranin, GFAP and NeuN negativity. What is the most likely diagnosis?

    1. Gangliocytoma
    2. Ganglioglioma
    3. Multinodular and vacuolating neuronal tumor (MVNT)
    4. Pilocytic astrocytoma
    5. Pleomorphic xanthoastrocytoma
    Board review style answer #1
    C. Multinodular and vacuolating neuronal tumor (MVNT). The image shows a neuronal tumor with a multinodular appearance and vacuolar changes in the cytoplasm of neoplastic neuronal elements and in the fibrillar matrix of the nodules. Answer D is incorrect because pilocytic astrocytoma has a completely different radiological presentation and morphological appearance, characterized by elongated glial neoplastic elements immersed in a fibrillary or loose background, usually associated with the presence of Rosenthal fibers. Answer B is incorrect because ganglioglioma shows larger neoplastic neurons (i.e., dysplastic ganglion cells), has a different immunohistochemical phenotype (positive for synaptophysin, chromogranin A, NeuN and NFP) and has a glial component. Answer A is incorrect because gangliocytoma is composed of numerous dysplastic ganglion cells, positive for synaptophysin, chromogranin A, NeuN and NFP (similar to ganglioglioma but without the glial component). Answer E is incorrect because pleomorphic xanthoastrocytoma shows a different clinical and histological appearance, characterized by pleomorphic neoplastic elements with spindle or epithelioid morphology, frequently with xanthomatous cytoplasm.

    Comment Here

    Reference: Multinodular and vacuolating neuronal tumor (MVNT)
    Board review style question #2
    Which of the following is an essential criterion to confirm a diagnosis of multinodular and vacuolating neuronal tumor (MVNT)?

    1. Biphasic tumor composed of neuronal and glial elements
    2. BRAF p.V600E mutation
    3. MAPK pathway activating alterations
    4. Neuronal cytological features or immunoreactivity for specific neuronal markers
    5. Olig2 positivity
    Board review style answer #2
    D. Neuronal cytological features or immunoreactivity for specific neuronal markers. According to the 2021 CNS WHO criteria, demonstration of neuronal cytology or immunoreactivity for specific neuronal markers (synaptophysin, HuC / HuD or nonphosphorylated 200 kDa NFP) is mandatory to confirm a diagnosis of MVNT. Answers C and E are incorrect because MAPK pathway activation alterations and Olig2 positivity are desirable but not essential criteria. Answer B is incorrect because BRAF p.V600E mutation has not been documented in MVNT, unlike other BRAF alterations. Answer A is incorrect because MVNT is only composed of neoplastic neuronal elements, without any associated neoplastic glial component.

    Comment Here

    Reference: Multinodular and vacuolating neuronal tumor (MVNT)
